explanation: The requirement states that when prop_0 occurs, then the system must (prop_1) all of the following: prop_2, prop_3, communication between controller and sensors (say, prop_4), communication between controller and valves (prop_5), and communication between controller and GUI (prop_6). If we assume each communication is an atomic proposition as per the list, the requirement is that after prop_0, eventually all the conditions (prop_1 & prop_2 & prop_3 & prop_4 & prop_5 & prop_6) hold at the same time. This can be written as: 'Whenever prop_0 happens, eventually all the required props are true.'
output_LTL: G ((StartUpMode) -> F ((initStart) & (loadLastParams) & (selfTestPass) & (selfTestPass & checkCommsSensors & checkCommsValves) & (selfTestPass & checkCommsSensors) & (checkCommsSensors & checkCommsValves)))

explanation: The requirement says that when Start-up Mode is initiated (prop_0), then prop_1, prop_2, controller-sensor communication (prop_3), controller-valve communication (prop_4), and controller-GUI communication (let's use prop_3 and prop_4 generically if no specific atoms are given) should all hold. If 'the communication of the controller with the sensors and valves, as well as between the controller and the GUI' is all included within available propositions, and we only have prop_0~prop_4, then prop_3 and prop_4 most likely correspond to those communications. So, after prop_0 occurs, prop_1, prop_2, prop_3, and prop_4 must all hold. The phrase 'upon initiation' is usually modeled as: Globally, if prop_0 occurs, then (prop_1 & prop_2 & prop_3 & prop_4) must hold (either immediately or from then on, depending on domain). If they must all begin to hold at the next step, we use X. If they must hold at the exact same step as prop_0, then we embed all in one step with implication. Here, I'll use implication: G (prop_0 -> (prop_1 & prop_2 & prop_3 & prop_4))

The statement says that when Start-up Mode begins (let's represent this as 'prop_0'), the system must ensure prop_1 and prop_2 hold, as well as the communication between the controller and the sensors/valves and the controller and the GUI. Since only the atomic propositions prop_0, prop_1, prop_2, prop_3, and prop_4 are allowed, and the communication events should be mapped to available atomic props (assume prop_3 = controller-sensors/valves comms and prop_4 = controller-GUI comms). We express: Whenever prop_0 occurs, it guarantees prop_1, prop_2, prop_3, and prop_4 at that time.

Whenever prop_0 holds, it is required that prop_1 occurs and that both prop_2, prop_3, prop_4, and prop_5 hold together at some point in the future. This can be captured by stating that globally, if prop_0 occurs, then eventually prop_1 and (prop_2 & prop_3 & prop_4 & prop_5) all occur.

The requirement is that upon entering 'Start-up Mode' (represented as prop_0), the system ensures prop_1, prop_2, and prop_3 are true, as well as communication ('between the controller and the GUI'). In the absence of an explicit proposition for the controller-GUI communication, we stick to the listed atomic propositions. So we encode that whenever prop_0 becomes true, then prop_1, prop_2, and prop_3 must all hold at that moment. This is best represented as: globally, if prop_0, then prop_1 & prop_2 & prop_3.

This requirement specifies that whenever prop_0 occurs, the system must respond with prop_1, performed in a manner that ensures prop_2, prop_3, and prop_4 are all true, as well as prop_5. In LTL, 'upon prop_0, the system shall...' usually translates to a global constraint: whenever prop_0 happens, subsequently prop_1 & prop_2 & prop_3 & prop_4 & prop_5 must hold.

The requirement specifies that when prop_0 occurs, the system must ensure prop_1 holds, and prop_2, prop_3, prop_4, and prop_5 all hold as well. This can be translated as: globally, if prop_0 holds, then prop_1, prop_2, prop_3, prop_4, and prop_5 all hold together at that point.

The requirement says that upon occurrence of prop_0, the system must ensure prop_1, which itself represents the fulfillment of prop_2, prop_3, and also two communications (which must also be representable in LTL using available atomic propositions). Since only prop_0 to prop_5 are provided, it's likely that prop_2 and prop_3 refer to their respective atomic propositions and the communications are also captured by prop_4 and prop_5. The prefix 'upon prop_0' translates to 'globally, if prop_0 occurs, then ...'. The rest, '[the system] shall prop_1 that (...and...and...and...)', is a bit ambiguous, but since only atomic propositions can be used, we represent 'prop_1 that (prop_2 and prop_3 and ...)' as (prop_1 & prop_2 & prop_3 & prop_4 & prop_5), assuming prop_4 = communication controller <-> sensors/valves, prop_5 = communication controller <-> GUI. Thus, the LTL formula is: G(prop_0 -> (prop_1 & prop_2 & prop_3 & prop_4 & prop_5)).

The sentence structure 'Upon prop_0, the prop_1 that prop_2 and prop_3 and prop_4, as well as prop_5.' expresses that whenever prop_0 happens, at some future point prop_1 must occur, and when prop_1 occurs, prop_2, prop_3 and prop_4 all hold at that moment, and prop_5 also holds. This translates to: globally, if prop_0, then eventually (prop_1 and, at the same time, prop_2, prop_3, prop_4, and prop_5).

This requirement specifies that whenever prop_0 occurs, the system must ensure (prop_1) that all of prop_2, prop_3, prop_4, and prop_5 hold. The phrase 'the system shall ... that ...' can be interpreted as a requirement that upon the triggering condition (prop_0), the system must respond to make true the conjunction of the listed propositions. In LTL, this is modeled by an implication: globally, whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 and all listed atomic propositions must also hold at that moment.
